content:
I saw there's a file that implement linked list in the kernel called linked_list.rs, but because they don't implement the getLinks trait, I can't store them there.
I also tried using unsafe_list.rs but it needs an Adapter trait in the objects that are stored and I don't really understand what this trait does.
I also tried creating an array of vectors but once I declare the arrays I can't modify them, and I don't really remember why.
Everything I'm talking about now is about a modification of wedson's scull module, which, by following the tutorial, is capped with 4MB of size, and I'd like more capacity to compare it to its C counterpart

Is it theoretically possible to implement the GetLinksWrapped for an array of arrays as scull traditionally does? I don't really know what does this trait do. The code might look like this... 

struct arrayLink{
    data: [Option<[u8;4096]>;1024],
}
impl GetLinksWrapped for arrayLink{
    ...
}

This may be a good option since I wouldn't edit the code for the vectors for them to implement this trait
Can you explain in more detail what you want? I don't really understand what you mean by a vector in a linked list.

I'd like to store vectors in a the list defined in linked_list.rs, but the list elements must implement the GetLinksWrapped trait so I can't store them. I want a data structure that can hold many vectors to have a device with more than 4MB of capacity.
The ultimate goal is to get more space in the device since dd command reports a failure when 4MB of data are written into the device file

Here's an example taken from binder:

struct Descriptor<T> {
    state: DescriptorState,
    size: usize,
    offset: usize,
    links: Links<Descriptor<T>>,
    data: Option<T>,
}

impl<T> Descriptor<T> {
    fn new(offset: usize, size: usize) -> Self {
        Self {
            size,
            offset,
            state: DescriptorState::Free,
            links: Links::new(),
            data: None,
        }
    }
}

impl<T> GetLinks for Descriptor<T> {
    type EntryType = Self;
    fn get_links(desc: &Self) -> &Links<Self> {
        &desc.links
    }
}
Make a struct that contains your array and a Links field, then do the same.
Would this also work with the getLinksWrapped trait? And, is the "links" element in the struct the next item in the linked list? I'm probably getting it wrong though
As expected, it doesn't work, could you or someone send me a link to the code you show above?

Also, about the error that appears when the vector tries to be more than 4MB big, do you know if there's a restriction about the maximum of adjacent memory that a program can have? I've been seeing the implementation of Vec::try_resize and it seems that it uses adjacent memory to write the data.

Alphyto said:

Also, about the error that appears when the vector tries to be more than 4MB big, do you know if there's a restriction about the maximum of adjacent memory that a program can have? I've been seeing the implementation of Vec::try_resize and it seems that it uses adjacent memory to write the data.

kmalloc is limited to 4MB per object, yes. If you need to manage large amounts of memory in the kernel you'll probably need something more custom/specific that directly allocates pages...
But if you just want to work around the limitation you could just have a Vec of Vecs?
